content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 10, 2018

Theresa E. Fuentes
Assistant City Attorney
City of Pasadena
100 N. Garfield Avenue, #N-210
Pasadena, CA 91101

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-17-276

Dear Ms. Fuentes:

        This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Pasadena City Councilmember
Steve Madison regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

        Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

        Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Madison from taking part in governmental decisions
relating to the City of Pasadena's potential regulation of short-term rentals within the City given
that the Councilmember is a partner at a law firm that recently represented Airbnb, a privately held
businesses that provides an online marketplace facilitating short-term rentals for a fee?

CONCLUSION

        No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions
because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any of his financial
interests based on the facts presented.

_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-276
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are an Assistant City Attorney and the authorized representative of Councilmember
Madison. The City Council will soon consider whether to regulate short-term rentals of residential
properties within the City.

Airbnb is a privately held business that provides an online marketplace facilitating short-
term rentals for a fee. Airbnb does not own the residential real properties offered for short-term
rental. Rather, Airbnb is a broker of short-term rentals that receives commissions from both guests
and hosts in conjunction with every booking. The cost of each short-term rental is set by the host.
According to the Wall Street Journal, Airbnb was valued at $30 billion in September 2016.
According to its website, Airbnb has over 200,000,000 users, and over 3,000,000 short term rental
listings worldwide, with listings in over 65,000 cities and 191 countries.

The Councilmember is a partner at the law firm of Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan
("Quinn Emanuel"), a multi-national law firm with thousands of clients worldwide. As a partner,
the Councilmember receives a small percentage of Quinn Emanuel's profits, but does not have a
10-percent or greater ownership interest in the firm. Quinn Emanuel recently represented Airbnb in
an employment matter and received more than $500 for this work. The Councilmember did not
work on that matter, and Airbnb is no longer a client of Quinn Emanuel.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from taking part in a governmental decision that
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's
financial interests identified in Section 87103. Of those interests, the Councilmember's interests
implicated by the decisions relating to the City's potential regulation of short-term rentals and the
facts presented are the following:

Business Interest

A public official has an interest in any business entity in which he has a direct or indirect
investment worth $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)), or in which he is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, or employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103 (d)). The
Councilmember has a business interest in Quinn Emanuel because he is a partner of the firm.

The Councilmember does not have a business interest in Airbnb under Section 87103(a) or
(d) because the facts presented provide no indication that the Councilmember has any investment,
or holds any position of management in, Airbnb.

Source of Income Interest

A public official has an interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value
provided or promised to, or received by, the official within 12 months prior to when the decisions is
made. (Section 87103(c).) The Councilmember has a source of income interest in Quinn Emanuel
because he has received $500 or more in income from the firm within the 12 months preceding the
decisions at issue.

File No. A-17-276
Page No. 3

Section 82030 sets forth the definition of "income" for purposes of the Act, and subdivision
(a) of that section provides that "Income of a des a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater."

Based on the facts presented, the Councilmember does not own a 10-percent interest or
greater in Quinn Emmanuel. Therefore, the Councilmember does not have a source of income interest
in clients or former clients of Quinn Emmanuel, including Airbnb.

Interest in Personal Finances

An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate
family members. (See Section 87103.)

Thus, the Councilmember has financial interests in Quinn Emmanuel and his own personal
finances and those of immediate family members, and does not have a financial interest in Airbnb,
based on the facts presented.

Foreseeability

Regulation 18701 sets forth the Act's standards for determining whether a decision's
financial effect on an official's financial interest is reasonably foreseeable. Subdivision (a) of that
provision provides that an official's interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a
named party in or the subject of the decision, and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if it
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. Neither Quinn Emmanuel nor the Councilmember's
personal finances are explicitly involved in the decisions relating to the City's potential regulation
of short-term rentals within the City.

Regulation 18701(b) provides the foreseeability standard applicable to an official's interest
which is not explicitly involved in the decisions at issue:

"In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility
and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the
financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

There is no indication from the facts presented that City Council decisions relating to the
potential regulation of short-term rentals within the City will have any financial effect on Quinn
Emanuel. Airbnb is no longer a client of the firm, and there is no indication that these decisions
would have any financial effect on any of the firm's current clients or the firm itself. Furthermore,
there is no indication that these decisions would have any effect on the Councilmember's personal
finances. Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking
part in decisions relating to the potential regulation of short-term rentals within the City because
those decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any of his financial
interests based on the facts presented.

File No. A-17-276
Page No. 4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Madison from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to the City of Pasadena's potential regulation of short-term rentals within the City given 
that the Councilmember is a partner at a law firm that recently represented Airbnb, a privately held 
business that provides an online marketplace facilitating short-term rentals for a fee?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions 
because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any of his financial 
interests based on the facts presented.

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from taking part in a governmental decision that 
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's 
financial interests identified in Section 87103. Of those interests, the Councilmember' s interests 
implicated by the decisions relating to the City's potential regulation of short-tenn rentals and the 
facts presented are the following:

A public official has an interest in any business entity in which he has a direct or indirect

Councilmember has a business interest in Quinn Emanuel because he is a partner of the finn.

(d) because the facts presented provide no indication that the Councilmember has any investment, 
or holds any position of management in, Airbnb. 
Source of Income Interest 
A public official has an interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value 
provided or promised to, or received by, the official within 12 months prior to when the decisions is 
made. (Section 87103(c).) The Councilmember has a source of income interest in Quinn Emanuel 
because he has received $500 or more in income from the finn within the 12 months preceding the 
decisions at issue.  
Section 82030 sets forth the definition of"income" for purposes of the Act, and subdivision 
(a) of that section provides that "Income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any 
income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or 
beneficially, a I 0-percent interest or greater." 
Based on the facts presented, the Councilmember does not own a I 0-percent interest or 
greater in Quinn Emanuel. Therefore, the Councilmember does not have a source of income interest 
in clients or former clients of Quinn Emanuel, including Airbnb. 
Interest in Personal Finances 
An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate 
family members. (See Section 87103.) 
Thus, the Councilmember has financial interests in Quinn Emanuel and his own personal 
finances and those of immediate family members, and does not have a financial interest in Airbnb, 
based on the facts presented. 
Foreseeability 
Regulation 18701 sets forth the Act's standards for determining whether a decision's 
financial effect on an official's financial interest is reasonably foreseeable. Subdivision (a) of that 
provision provides that an official's interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a 
named party in or the subject of the decision, and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if it

entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. Neither Quinn Emanuel nor the Councilmember's 
personal finances are explicitly involved in the decisions relating to the City's potential regulation 
of short-term rentals within the City. 
Regulation 1870l(b) provides the foreseeability standard applicable to an official's interest 
which is not explicitly involved in the decisions at issue: 
"In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility 
and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the 
financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not 
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." 
There is no indication from the facts presented that City Council decisions relating to the 
potential regulation of short-term rentals within the City will have any financial effect on Quinn 
Emanuel. Airbnb is no longer a client of the firm, and there is no indication that these decisions 
would have a financial effect on any of the firm's current clients or the firm itself. Furthermore, 
there is no indication that these decisions would have any effect on the Councilmember's personal 
finances. Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking 
part in decisions relating to the potential regulation of short-term rentals within the City because 
those decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any of his financial 
interests based on the facts presented.
